did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble transfer learning approach (combining AlexNet, VGG, DenseNet, and ResNet) coupled with an Osprey Optimization Algorithm (OOA) for feature selection and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to perform skin cancer classification on the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'Ensemble Transfer Learning coupled Osprey Optimization Algorithm' for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (feature extraction using AlexNet, VGG, DenseNet, ResNet; feature selection via OOA; classification via SVM), describes the dataset used (ISIC with 24,225 images), and reports new experimental results (98.61% accuracy) compared against state-of-the-art models. Although it includes a literature survey section, its primary purpose is to report new technical contributions and validation, not to summarize prior literature.

title: Ensemble Transfer Learning Coupled Osprey Optimization Algorithm for Feature Selection from Skin Cancer Image Datasets
journal: 2025 Fourth International Conference on Smart Technologies, Communication and Robotics (STCR)
brief_summary: This study proposes an ensemble transfer learning approach coupled with an Osprey Optimization Algorithm (OOA) for feature selection to classify skin cancer images. The method utilizes pre-trained models including AlexNet, VGG, DenseNet, and ResNet for primary feature extraction, followed by OOA to select optimal features and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification. The model was evaluated on the ISIC public repository dataset, which contained 24,225 images collected between 2019 and 2020. To address class imbalance, a resampling technique was applied, splitting the data into 19,378 training images and 4,632 test images. The experimental results demonstrated high performance, achieving a diagnostic accuracy of 98.61%, precision of 96.00%, sensitivity of 100%, and specificity of 97.62%. These results indicate that the proposed Ensemble-OOA model outperforms several existing state-of-the-art counterparts in skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on classifying skin cancer images using the ISIC dataset. While the dataset contains specific diagnostic labels (e.g., melanoma thickness is mentioned as available metadata), the study treats the task as a binary or general 'skin cancer' classification problem without explicitly listing or separating specific histological subtypes (like melanoma vs. BCC) in the experimental results or model evaluation.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnosis (binary classification of skin cancer images)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to evaluate the performance of the ensemble transfer learning model coupled with Osprey Optimization Algorithm. The task is described as 'skin cancer classification' and 'diagnosis of skin cancer'. The dataset contains labels for skin cancer, and the model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (presence of skin cancer). Performance metrics reported include accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Public Repository Dataset
datasets_used.description: A large-scale skin cancer image dataset collected from the ISIC public repository (isic-archive.com). The images were captured by dermatologists between 2019 and 2020. The dataset includes metadata such as disease position, age, sex, diagnosis history, and melanoma thickness.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble transfer learning models (AlexNet, VGG, DenseNet, ResNet) and the Support Vector Machine classifier after applying a resampling technique to address class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing the trained model to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity).
use.other: Used for feature extraction and feature selection via the Osprey Optimization Algorithm (OOA).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity) and compared against other models in a table without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Public Repository Dataset.notes: The proposed Ensemble-OOA model outperformed individual TL models (AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, DenseNet) and the basic Ensemble model. The paper also compared the proposed model against state-of-the-art methods from literature (Table II), noting that reference [21] achieved 98.86% accuracy on a different dataset size (10,015 images), while the proposed model achieved 98.61% on the 24,225 image dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble Transfer Learning coupled with Osprey Optimization Algorithm (OOA) and Support Vector Machine (SVM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models: AlexNet, VGG (VGG-16/VGG-19), ResNet (ResNet-50/ResNet-101), and DenseNet (DenseNet-169/DenseNet201). Initial layers were frozen.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Features extracted independently from each pre-trained model and concatenated to form a combined feature matrix.
architecture_details.feature_selection: Osprey Optimization Algorithm (OOA) used to select optimal features from the combined feature subset.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (SVM used for classification)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble Transfer Learning coupled Osprey Optimization Algorithm (Ensemble-OOA)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization to match pretrained model requirements
Resampling technique to address class imbalance in the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: A resampling technique was applied to the dataset to address class imbalance, splitting the data into 19,378 training images and 4,632 test images.

conclusions: The proposed Ensemble Transfer Learning coupled with Osprey Optimization Algorithm (Ensemble-OOA) achieved a diagnostic accuracy of 98.61%, precision of 96.00%, sensitivity of 100%, and specificity of 97.62% on the ISIC skin cancer dataset.
The Ensemble-OOA model outperformed individual transfer learning models (AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, DenseNet) and a basic ensemble model without feature selection.

The use of the Osprey Optimization Algorithm (OOA) for feature selection effectively reduced noise from the features extracted by transfer learning algorithms, thereby improving classifier performance.

The proposed model demonstrated superior performance compared to several state-of-the-art counterparts reported in the literature, such as those achieving 85.00%, 90.00%, 86.90%, 95.50%, and 89.57% accuracy.
The study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in skin cancer datasets by applying a resampling technique, splitting the 24,225 images into 19,378 training and 4,632 test images.

The research contributes a novel CAD model architecture that enhances the efficacy of basic CNN models through ensemble transfer learning and optimal feature selection, aiming to assist dermatologists in early skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study uses a binary classification task (skin cancer vs. non-skin cancer) rather than multi-class histological subtyping (e.g., melanoma vs. BCC), limiting clinical granularity.

The dataset (ISIC) contains images collected between 2019 and 2020; the paper does not report performance on more recent data or external validation cohorts, raising concerns about temporal and geographic bias.

Class imbalance was addressed via resampling, but the specific technique (e.g., SMOTE, random oversampling/undersampling) is not detailed, which may introduce data leakage or synthetic bias if not handled carefully.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed model over state-of-the-art counterparts.
The proposed model achieved 100% sensitivity, which may indicate overfitting or an optimistic bias given the lack of external validation and statistical testing.

Comparison with state-of-the-art methods is limited by inconsistent dataset sizes and potentially different preprocessing pipelines in cited works (e.g., reference [21] used 10,015 images vs. 24,225 in this study), making direct performance comparisons difficult.
The paper is a conference publication with limited scope for extensive ablation studies or hyperparameter sensitivity analysis compared to journal standards.
The use of a single GPU (NVIDIA GTX 1080Ti) and Python implementation suggests computational constraints that may not reflect scalability in clinical deployment environments.
